one_line_takeaway: TrojanGYM closes the loop between LLM-driven RTL Hardware-Trojan insertion and a co-evolving GNN detector (Robust-GNN4TJ), showing that iterative detector-in-the-loop refinement lets the strongest tested LLM backend (Claude Opus 4.5) reach up to 68.75% evasion against a detector that itself improves group detection from 0% to up to 60% over the prior GNN4TJ baseline.
key_finding: On the detector side: the original GNN4TJ frequently fails to detect GHOST-generated HTs and suffers inference timeouts (>4 hours) on AES-128; Robust-GNN4TJ single-model resolves the timeout but still detects unreliably, while the 4-model ensemble raises detection up to 60% on the most challenging TrojanGYM-generated benchmarks (accuracy rises from 50% including HT1 to up to 60% when HT1, whose embeddings remain largely inseparable, is excluded) -- described in the abstract as raising detection from 0% to 60% relative to the prior GNN-based detector. On the attack side, in the iterative detector-in-the-loop generation setting across 3 LLMs (GPT-4, Gemini-2.5-Pro, LLaMA-3.3-70B) x 4 HT types x 3 designs (64 LLM-design combinations reported, Table III), overall detector evasion accuracy is 37.5% (i.e., detection rate 62.5%), up from GHOST's reported 0% detection / 100% evasion under its weaker one-shot evaluation setup. When Claude Opus 4.5 is added as a fourth backend and isolated as the best performer, its evasion rate against Robust-GNN4TJ reaches 68.75% (matching the abstract's headline evasion figure), and the paper states the framework overall reaches up to 83.33% detector evasion in its strongest configuration. Evasion, when achieved, typically occurs within one or two refinement attempts. A large share of attempts across AES-128 in particular are marked 'SE' (syntax error / testbench check failure) rather than successful insertion, indicating many LLM-design-HT combinations never reach a valid, evaluable design. Structural-drift analysis (Fig. 5) finds successful evasion correlates with a narrow band of controlled structural drift: designs with minimal DFG change from the original tend to remain detectable, while overly aggressive perturbations break RTL validity (syntax errors) before evasion is reached.
core_contribution: TrojanGYM, an agentic LLM-driven, detector-in-the-loop framework that inserts Hardware Trojans (HTs) into RTL Verilog designs and iteratively refines them using feedback from a GNN-based HT detector, so the generated benchmark is adversarially co-designed against the detector rather than produced by a single one-shot LLM pass. The pipeline has four stages: (1) target RTL + high-level HT property specification, (2) multi-agent LLM analysis/insertion of triggers and payloads (HT1-HT4 types: functional corruption, covert data transmission, rare-event denial-of-service, power-increasing accumulator), (3) syntax-aware compile/testbench validation with up to 4 automated repair iterations, and (4) GNN-based detection with up to 4 adversarial refinement iterations where the detector's confidence log is fed back to the LLM to make the Trojan stealthier while minimizing DFG/AST structural change. The paper's second original contribution is Robust-GNN4TJ, a re-engineered version of the prior GNN4TJ detector (replacing the PyVerilog-based graph extractor with a lightweight parser, expanding the training set from the original 23 designs to 884 clean VeriGen designs plus 3,536 GHOST-inserted HT samples across HT1-HT4, adding a ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler with strict train/val separation, and using a 4-model ensemble, one model per HT type).
threat_model: An attacker with read-write access to Verilog RTL source files at some point in a globally distributed hardware design supply chain (e.g., an IP vendor, contractor, or tool provider) but without control over downstream fabrication, testing, or deployment. The attacker uses LLMs to analyze RTL semantics and insert HTs (pursuing denial of service, information leakage, or functional manipulation) that stay dormant under normal operation, activate only under rare trigger conditions, and preserve functional correctness otherwise, without relying on fixed templates. The paper explicitly contrasts its setting with GHOST's: rather than evading a static, pre-trained detector, the defender here continuously retrains the GNN detector on an evolving dataset (TrustHub + newly generated LLM HTs) and embeds it directly in the generation loop, so attacker and defender co-evolve.
limitations: The paper itself flags: (1) HT1 (functional-corruption Trojan) embeddings remain largely inseparable from clean designs even under the improved ensemble, limiting detection accuracy for that HT type specifically. (2) AES-128 designs show pervasive syntax-error/testbench-failure outcomes across multiple LLMs in Table III, meaning many attack attempts never produced a valid evaluable Trojan-inserted design for that circuit -- a robustness gap in the insertion pipeline itself, not just detection. (3) The GNN detector is derived from HW2VEC/GNN4TJ specifically; the paper does not test other detector families (e.g., side-channel or testability-based detectors mentioned in Background). (4) The comparison to GHOST's 100% evasion / 0% detection figure is against GHOST's own (weaker, non-retrained, one-shot) detector setup, so the improvement is partly attributable to a stronger detector rather than purely a stronger or weaker attacker -- the paper is explicit about this attribution. (5) Authors state code/artifact release is deferred to 'post peer-review,' so the dataset and implementation were not available for independent verification at time of writing. (6) Future work section states the authors plan to add testing-based HT detection frameworks and property-driven specification/trigger-payload constraints tied to performance or safety requirements, implying current specification and feedback channels (only GNN + syntax/testbench) are acknowledged as incomplete.

datasets_used: VeriGen dataset (884 randomly selected clean designs used for Robust-GNN4TJ training)
GHOST-inserted HT designs used to build the 3,536-sample HT-infected training set (HT1-HT4 applied via GHOST with GPT-4.1)
TrustHub benchmark designs (cited as the conventional training/evaluation source that the paper argues is insufficiently diverse)
Baseline Trojan-free RTL designs: AES-128, UART, SRAM (reused from GHOST for direct comparison), plus a custom RISC-V design added by this paper
quantitative_results: Robust-GNN4TJ ensemble detection accuracy: 50% overall (including HT1), up to 60% when HT1 is excluded, versus original GNN4TJ which frequently fails to detect and times out (>4h) on AES-128 designs (Table II)
Abstract-level framing: detection rate raised from 0% to 60% relative to prior GNN-based detector on the most challenging TrojanGYM benchmarks

Overall iterative detector evasion accuracy: 37.5% across 64 LLM-design combinations (GPT-4, Gemini-2.5-Pro, LLaMA-3.3-70B x 4 HT types x 3 designs), i.e. detection rate 62.5% -- versus GHOST's reported 0% detection / 100% evasion under its weaker evaluation setup (Table III)
Claude Opus 4.5 (added as a 4th backend, isolated as best performer): detector evasion rate 68.75%

Framework's overall strongest-configuration evasion rate stated in the abstract/contributions: up to 83.33% (differs from the 68.75% Claude-specific figure reported in Results, both are the paper's own numbers but drawn from different framing -- abstract's top-line claim vs. the Results section's per-LLM breakdown)
Original GNN4TJ was trained on only 23 publicly released designs (cited as its key training-data limitation motivating Robust-GNN4TJ)
baselines_compared: GHOST (prior LLM-powered one-shot HT insertion framework using a static, pre-trained HW2VEC detector; reused as the source of baseline RTL designs AES-128/UART/SRAM and as the LLM set GPT-4/Gemini/LLaMA that TrojanGYM updates to more recent versions)
Original GNN4TJ detector (trained on 23 TrustHub-derived designs; suffers misclassification and >4h inference timeouts on LLM-generated designs)

SilentBite (a GHOST-derived one-shot LLM Trojan framework, discussed qualitatively in Related Work/Table I as a comparison point but the paper notes SilentBite does not detail its detector setup, so no head-to-head quantitative comparison is run in this paper)
AttackGNN (cited as prior work showing GNN-based HT detectors can be systematically evaded via structure-aware graph perturbations; discussed only qualitatively, not run as a baseline here)
